title: Chapter 24: The Heavenly Hall
paragraphs: Death in a fading world is a commonplace phenomenon. It hides in the radioactive mist, lurks in the mandibles of monsters, and settles as gray dust on the lips of exhausted refugees. Usually, it comes unnoticed, erasing lives as the wind erases tracks on the sand.
But the death of the goblin from the Rotten Hollow stopped time.

The silence that descended upon the Gates of Etcha was heavy and monumental. Not a person uttered a word when, from under the vaults of the city, breaking all thousand-year-old taboos, High Elder Elarion emerged.

He left his sterile marble tomb of the White Hall. The old man went on foot, without an entourage, his silver-embroidered clothes dragging through the mud, soaking up the gray ash of the wastelands. Elarion approached the crater where the elven guards, the weeping giant demon, the werewolf, and the fox knelt.

The High Elder stopped before Yashil's body. The great elf slowly raised his trembling hands to his head. He took off his tiara—the crown of a ruler, forged from pure stellar metal—and, kneeling, carefully placed it on the frozen stone at the bare feet of the little goblin.

It was the end of an era. The wall of hatred that divided nations had not crumbled from magic. It was broken by a heart that for seven years refused to hate and for seven years served those the world deemed trash.

Rene approached the body. The fox, whose eyes were red from tears, gracefully crouched down. She took out her last mint cigarette. She flicked her lighter, took a draw, and placed it next to the tiara.
"Your move, Green," Rene whispered. "For seven years you read me poetry about hope. You won. We are no longer afraid."

Dzhanavar followed. The huge werewolf dropped to all fours. All his forced, lazy bravado had vanished. Dzhanavar tilted his muzzle to the sky and let out a long, drawn-out, soul-tearing wolf howl. And when the howl ceased, the werewolf exhaled:
"Auf, brother. Rest in peace."

Lord Valthor watched them from afar. The immortal vampire placed his hand over his heart and bowed deeply. He vanished into the mist, carrying the memory of these seven years as the greatest of his treasures.
For Yashil, pain no longer existed.
He opened his eyes. The first thing he realized was that the smell of rotting ozone had vanished. The air was unimaginably clean. It smelled of morning dew, pine needles, and spring.
Yashil stood on a path paved with shimmering mana. He was in the Heavenly Hall—a place where the forces of the universe carefully preserved the memory of the greatest souls of all past eras.

Ahead towered colossal arches of light. Under their vaults, Yashil saw thousands of tall silhouettes. The spirits of ancient heroes. The first kings of the High Elves, legendary dwarven smiths, protectors of the world.
They stood along the path, silently looking at the tiny figure of the goblin.
Yashil stopped timidly. All his life he had known his place. He was 'filth.' Instinct told him to lower his head and turn off the path into the shadow.
But the spirits did not look away.

From the shining crowd, a tall, majestic silhouette stepped forward. It was the spirit of the very first founder of Etcha. The ancient elven king approached the little goblin. His face was filled with absolute reverence.
The great king of the past did not draw his sword. He slowly knelt on one knee, bowing his head before the creature from the Rotten Hollow.

"You carried upon your fragile shoulders that which broke our steel-clad backs," the voice of the ancient spirit sounded like the chiming of crystal bells. "We built empires. We performed great deeds in days. But you—you performed thousands of unseen miracles for seven years, every day choosing love where we chose steel. You fed enemies, you reconciled killers, you became a light in a world that had already forgotten what the sun was. You destroyed the walls of our arrogance when others simply destroyed city walls."

One by one, the spirits of the greatest heroes stepped aside. They bowed their heads in recognition. They did not celebrate his death. They celebrated his seven-year Service—a path that proved longer and harder than any war.
Yashil slowly walked along the path of light. He was simply happy.

At the very end of the hall stood a female figure. She was short, with green skin. She was no longer the exhausted woman from the damp burrow. She glowed with a quiet strength that surpassed the radiance of kings.
"Mama..." Yashil breathed.
The little goblin rushed to her, and she knelt, holding her son tightly forever. His journey was complete.
The world of the living continued to wither. But in Etcha, something had changed irrevocably.

At the Great Gates, on the site of the crater, a statue of simple basalt was erected. A small figure of a goblin leaning on an iron pipe and looking into the sky. A fire always burned at the base of the statue.

His rest was guarded. A huge black demon sat in the shadow, protecting this place from the darkness. At night, a lone wolf howled on the parapet, and every morning, a fresh mint cigarette invariably appeared at the stone feet of the statue.
Far from Etcha, through the mist, a lonely traveler trudged.

Avotu walked heavily. He was alone in a dead world. But his back was straight. The black flame above his head gave no warmth. But in its core, a single ember had appeared. He carried the words of the goblin within him. The guilt over Biotrix's death had not gone away. The grief had not become lighter.

But now he had at least one reason to keep going. And he would walk through this rot until reality tore again and spilled five people toward him who have no magic, but are capable of standing shoulder to shoulder.
The story of the goblin ended, becoming a legend. The story of the Isekaiers and the Black Flame was only beginning.
